answers.field_label: How Long Have You Known This Trainer
answers.value: I have known Emily Rose Cartwright for just over 7 years, since early 2019, when she moved her first off-track Thoroughbred into my boarding facility in Loveland, Colorado. We initially met through a local Mustang and burro advocacy group, and over the years our relationship has grown from a simple boarder–barn-owner connection into a close professional and personal friendship. During this time, I have seen her progress from working mainly with lightly started horses to confidently and thoughtfully handling green, unhandled, and mustang-type horses. I have been consistently impressed with how she continues her education—attending clinics, reading, and consulting with more experienced trainers when she encounters a new challenge. Because we have known each other through multiple seasons, different horses, and a variety of situations (including health issues, weather emergencies, and hauling to events), I feel I have a very complete and realistic understanding of who she is as a trainer and caretaker.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: How Often Do You See This Trainer Ride Or Work Horses
answers.value:
Currently, I see Emily ride or work with horses 4–6 days per week, often multiple sessions per day when her schedule allows. She boards two of her own horses at my facility and regularly hauls in outside horses for groundwork and colt-starting sessions. On average, I am physically present at the barn for some portion of her work at least three or four days each week. On weekends, I often observe full training sessions from start to finish, including catching the horse, grooming, groundwork, riding (if appropriate for that horse’s stage), and cool-down. On weekday evenings, I frequently see at least part of her sessions—especially her groundwork with younger or greener horses. 

In addition, I have accompanied her as a ground person or extra set of hands at several events and clinics, including a local mustang demo day in Greeley in 2023 and a trail obstacle clinic at Sylvan Dale Ranch in 2024. This means I have seen her work in a variety of contexts: at home, at new facilities, in busy show environments, and on the trail. I also occasionally receive videos from her when she wants feedback on a specific training issue, so even when I am not there in person, I have a fairly complete picture of how consistently she approaches her training and horsemanship.

answers.field_label: Do You Feel That Their Facilities Are Suitable For Working With A Wild Horse
answers.value:
yes – and I can give quite a bit of detail. Emily does most of her training out of my facility, Jennings Creek Equestrian, located just outside Loveland, CO. We have specifically set up the property to safely accommodate green and unhandled horses, and Emily has been directly involved in how we organize and maintain the spaces she uses.

For initial gentling and handling of a wild or completely unhandled horse, Emily uses a heavy-duty, six-foot-tall steel pipe round pen (60 feet in diameter) with secure, well-maintained footing. The panels are solid and properly chained and clipped, with no loose or sharp edges. There are no low spots where a horse could get a hoof caught, and the footing is a mix of sand and fine screenings that drains well and does not get slick when wet. The round pen is located inside a larger fenced area, so if a horse were to slip a halter or push a gate, there is still a secondary barrier before they would reach any open space.

In addition to the round pen, Emily has access to:
- A set of solid-board pens with 6-foot fencing and secure latches for newly arrived or more reactive horses.
- A central barn aisle where she can safely tie or use cross-ties once the horse is ready for closer handling, grooming, and vet or farrier care.
- A well-maintained outdoor arena (approximately 100’ x 200’) that is fully fenced with safe, high rails and good footing, which she uses only after the horse is reliably halter broke and comfortable with basic leading.

The property has non-slip rubber mats in the grooming and wash areas, safe tying posts, and multiple options for safely loading and unloading from trailers. Emily is meticulous about checking gates, latches, and the condition of panels before working with any horse, and she has often been the one to point out needed repairs or improvements.

We also have a good relationship with a local equine veterinarian and a farrier who are both experienced with mustangs and unhandled horses, and Emily coordinates with them in a way that prioritizes safety and stress reduction for the horse. There is always fresh water available in all pens, and she is very particular about hay nets, feeders, and buckets being placed so that a frightened or reactive horse is unlikely to get tangled.

In my opinion, not only are the facilities themselves suitable for working with a wild horse, but Emily’s approach to using those facilities—such as introducing new horses gradually to different spaces and never rushing them into the big arena before they are ready—makes the environment especially appropriate and safe for TIP Challenge horses.

answers.field_label: Do You Have Any Concerns About The Horses In The Care Of This Trainer
answers.value:
no – I do not have any concerns about the welfare, safety, or general treatment of the horses in Emily’s care. If anything, my only mild “concern,” in a positive sense, is that she tends to put the horse’s physical and mental well-being ahead of her own time and convenience, sometimes to the point of making her own schedule more difficult than it needs to be.

To elaborate, Emily is extremely conscientious about:

1. **Body condition and nutrition:**
   She keeps her horses and client horses in healthy working condition—never thin, never obese. She regularly evaluates weight, muscle development, and coat condition and adjusts feed accordingly. I have personally seen her contact owners to suggest changes to hay type or quantity, and she documents these conversations. She also keeps salt, clean water, and appropriate forage in front of horses as much as the boarding situation allows.

2. **Health and veterinary care:**
   Emily is quick to notice and respond to health concerns. For example, in March 2024, she noticed a very subtle, intermittent lameness in a client’s green mare that the owner had not picked up on. She immediately stopped ridden work, switched to light groundwork, and recommended a vet evaluation. It turned out the mare had a mild hoof abscess that was easily treated because it was caught early. Emily was calm, organized, and thorough in communicating with both the vet and the owner.

3. **Mental and emotional welfare:**
   Emily is very attuned to stress, fear, and confusion in horses. She does not push them past their capacity in the name of “getting it done.” I have watched her walk away from a session when a horse was mentally overloaded, choosing instead to do a few minutes of simple, confidence-building tasks and then ending on a quiet note. She often says, “I want this horse to still like people when I’m done,” and her training reflects that philosophy.

4. **Safe handling and boundaries:**
   She is firm about manners and boundaries, which actually enhances safety and welfare. She doesn’t allow dangerous behavior like striking, biting, or crowding, but she addresses these behaviors through thoughtful, consistent training—not punishment or frustration. I have never seen her lose her temper, yell, or strike a horse out of anger. Her corrections are always purposeful and proportional.

If I ever had concerns, I would not hesitate to say so, especially since the work we do involves green and sometimes reactive horses. But in seven years of observing her closely, including during stressful or high-pressure situations (such as loading a reluctant horse into a trailer before a storm), I have not seen anything that made me uncomfortable about how she cares for or trains horses. I would trust her with any of my own horses without hesitation.

answers.field_label: Would You Recommend This Trainer To People With Unbroken Or Difficult Horses
answers.value:
yes – I would absolutely recommend Emily Cartwright to people with unbroken, untouched, or behaviorally challenging horses, including wild or recently gathered mustangs. In fact, I already do so on a regular basis as part of my role running Jennings Creek Equestrian.

My recommendation is based on several specific reasons and real situations I have personally witnessed:

1. **Calm, consistent demeanor:**
   Emily has a very steady presence around reactive or fearful horses. One example stands out: in 2022, she worked with a 4-year-old BLM mustang gelding that had a history of bolting and pulling away. The first few sessions were rough—this horse would whirl, try to leave, and was clearly anxious about any pressure on the halter. Emily never raised her voice or escalated the situation. Instead, she broke the work into very small, manageable steps, praising every try and releasing pressure quickly. Within a couple of weeks, that horse was leading quietly, yielding his hindquarters, and loading into a trailer without drama.

2. **Clear understanding of how horses learn:**
   She has a solid, practical grasp of pressure-and-release, timing, and how to shape behavior without overwhelming the horse. When working with unbroken horses, she focuses first on building trust and communication—basic leading, yielding to pressure, and accepting touch—before she ever thinks about saddles or riding. I have seen her gently back up and re-establish groundwork skills when a horse struggled with a new task under saddle, and the result was a calmer, more confident horse.

3. **Patience and realistic expectations for owners:**
   Emily is honest with owners about the time and consistency required for difficult or green horses. She does not promise unrealistic quick fixes. For example, she turned down a request from a local owner who wanted a “30-day miracle” for a very nervous mare with a history of bucking. Instead, she explained what a safer, more ethical training plan would look like and ultimately only took the mare on when the owner agreed to a longer-term, step-by-step approach.

4. **Safety-first approach:**
   With unbroken or difficult horses, safety has to come first—for the horse, the trainer, and anyone else handling the horse. Emily is disciplined about wearing appropriate gear, setting up safe working areas, and not cutting corners. She is also good at teaching owners how to handle their horses safely once they go home, which is often the missing piece that leads to problems later.

Because of all of this, I routinely recommend Emily to boarders at my barn and to people in the local community when they ask for help with untouched mustangs, older horses that never got a proper start, or animals with specific handling issues like trailer loading, leading, or standing for the farrier. I would have no hesitation at all recommending her specifically for a TIP Challenge horse that needs thoughtful, methodical gentling and starting.

answers.field_label: Personal Comments Regarding Skill Work Ethic Values Etc
answers.value:
Emily is one of the most thoughtful and ethical trainers I have had at my facility in nearly fifteen years of running a boarding and training operation. Her skills, work ethic, and personal values all line up in a way that makes her particularly well-suited for the kind of responsibility that comes with a TIP Challenge horse.

**Skill and feel with horses:**
Emily has what many horse people call “good feel”—that almost intuitive sense of when to apply pressure, when to release, and when to pause and let the horse process. I have watched her handle everything from a previously unhandled 3-year-old mustang to a sensitive off-track Thoroughbred and an older Quarter Horse with ingrained pushy habits. In each case, she adjusts her energy, body language, and timing to what that particular horse needs.

For example, with a very shut-down mare we had in 2023, Emily deliberately kept her sessions short and quiet, focusing on simple tasks like “head down,” “yield hindquarters,” and “follow the feel” on the lead rope. She spent nearly two weeks doing what might look like “very little” to an outsider, but the mare’s eyes softened, her breathing slowed, and she started volunteering connection instead of bracing. By the time Emily introduced the saddle, the mare trusted her enough that the process was uneventful rather than dramatic.

**Work ethic and reliability:**
Emily is unfailingly reliable. She shows up when she says she will, regardless of weather or inconvenience, and she communicates clearly if something truly unavoidable comes up. During the extreme cold snap in January 2025, she came out twice a day for several days in a row to check on the horses she was responsible for, even though it meant driving on icy roads and bundling up in sub-zero windchill. She checked water, adjusted blankets, and modified training sessions so that the horses stayed safe and comfortable.

She also keeps detailed notes on each horse—what they worked on, how they responded, any health or behavioral changes she noticed. She often sends owners written updates along with short video clips, which helps build trust and keeps everyone on the same page.

**Values and ethics:**
What stands out most to me is Emily’s commitment to doing right by the horse, even when that might not be the most convenient or financially lucrative choice. She is willing to tell an owner, kindly but firmly, that a particular horse is not a good fit for their goals, or that they need to adjust their expectations. She has, on more than one occasion, recommended that an owner pursue a veterinary workup instead of pushing through a behavior issue that might be pain-related.

She is also humble and open to learning. I have seen her attend clinics with more experienced mustang trainers, take notes, and then thoughtfully integrate what she learned into her own program. She does not pretend to know everything, and she is comfortable asking for advice when she encounters something new. That humility, combined with her solid foundation of skill, is part of what makes her safe and effective with challenging horses.

On a personal level, Emily is respectful, easy to communicate with, and supportive of the broader horse community. She has volunteered at local 4-H shows, helped beginners learn how to handle their horses more safely, and even donated a few training sessions to a local rescue that takes in neglected horses.

In summary, Emily is exactly the kind of trainer I want associated with my facility and with programs like the TIP Challenge. She is competent, patient, and deeply invested in the well-being and long-term success of the horses she works with. I recommend her without reservation for this opportunity.

persona.background_context:
This is Emily, a 33-year-old colt starter and gentling trainer in Loveland, Colorado, whose life is built around horses, weather forecasts, and a constantly buzzing phone full of client messages and vet updates. She lives in a small but cozy two-bedroom rental townhouse on the west side of Loveland with her partner, Sam, an ER nurse who works rotating shifts at UCHealth. Their place is modest—hand-me-down furniture, a kitchen table that once belonged to Emily’s parents—but the walls are lined with framed photos of horses she has worked with over the years, ribbons from local trail and obstacle events, and a big wall calendar color-coded for training sessions, vet appointments, Sam’s night shifts, and the occasional rare weekend getaway. Emily’s days start early; she’s usually up by 5:30 a.m., brewing strong coffee and checking the weather before driving out to Jennings Creek Equestrian, the boarding and training facility just outside town where she does most of her work.

Her routine revolves around the ebb and flow of training horses at different stages. Most mornings, she tackles the more mentally intense work first: newly arrived mustangs in the solid-board pens, untouched or minimally handled horses that need careful, quiet sessions in the six-foot steel round pen. She often schedules two or three focused groundwork sessions before noon, carefully spacing them so she can let horses process in between. Emily is meticulous about safety—double-checking latches, scanning the footing in the round pen, and taking an extra minute to test each gate before leading in a particularly reactive horse. Hazel and Rowan, her own horses boarded at Jennings Creek, usually get worked in the afternoons. Hazel is her steady, athletic partner for trail clinics and low-key local shows, while Rowan, a 6-year-old mustang gelding she adopted and brought along slowly, is her ongoing project and reminder of why she loves this kind of work. On lighter days, she’ll hack one of them out on the property’s perimeter trails or haul to nearby Sylvan Dale Ranch to school over trail obstacles.

Financially, Emily’s life is a balancing act. As a self-employed trainer, her income fluctuates seasonally and with the local economy. She keeps detailed notes and schedules on her phone and laptop, tracking each horse’s progress and billing owners monthly. Winters are leaner, so she supplements her income by doing evening barn chores a few days a week and occasionally pet-sitting for boarders who are out of town. She’s slowly building a reputation in northern Colorado as the person to call for untouched mustangs, older horses that never got a proper start, and animals with specific handling issues like trailer loading or farrier anxiety. Laura, the barn owner and her longtime friend, regularly refers new clients to her, and they’ve developed a comfortable rhythm—sharing responsibility during weather emergencies, splitting trailer-hauling duties to clinics, and backing each other up when family obligations or illness crop up.

Outside of work, Emily’s life is grounded in a small but solid circle of relationships. Her parents still live in Fort Collins, about 40 minutes away, and she drives up at least twice a month for Sunday dinner. Her dad loves hearing about the problem-solving side of her training, treating it like applied psychology, while her mom gently worries about the risks of working with green horses and insists she text when she gets home late. Her younger brother Jacob lives in Denver; they don’t see each other as often as they’d like, but they stay close through group chats and the occasional Avalanche game. At home, she and Sam juggle two demanding schedules—Sam’s irregular ER shifts and Emily’s early mornings and weekend clinics. They’ve worked out a rhythm where Emily handles most of the early morning dog walks for neighbors in the townhouse complex, while Sam takes on late-night grocery runs and meal prep on her days off. They’re talking about someday buying a small property with a few acres, but for now, the plan is to keep building savings and Emily’s client base.

Recently, Emily’s attention has been split between her usual training load and a few larger transitions and concerns. In January 2025, a brutal cold snap hit northern Colorado, dropping windchills well below zero. Emily spent several days driving back and forth to Jennings Creek twice a day, checking blankets, breaking ice in water tanks, and modifying training sessions so no horse got too cold or stressed. That stretch left her exhausted but also reaffirmed her sense of responsibility; she slept better at night knowing she had physically laid eyes on every horse she was responsible for. Around the same time, she and Sam started seriously discussing Sam’s potential move to a more regular day-shift schedule in the ER, which would mean a pay cut but more predictable time together. The decision weighs on them—more time together would be a gift, but Emily knows that the extra financial margin from Sam’s current shift differentials helps cushion the unpredictability of her own income.

Day to day, Emily’s worries are the quiet, practical ones: making sure a nervous mare’s subtle lameness is fully resolved before resuming under-saddle work; planning how to fit in two new client horses arriving in late March without overloading herself; keeping her aging Subaru Outback running reliably for hauling her small two-horse trailer to clinics and vet appointments. She also keeps a close eye on her own body—aching shoulders from years of handling young horses, the occasional tweak in her lower back. She’s started doing yoga at home a few mornings a week, following YouTube videos before heading to the barn, and she’s trying to be more disciplined about stretching after long days on her feet.

Despite the long hours and physical toll, Emily is genuinely content with the life she’s built. She enjoys teaching a handful of dedicated amateur owners on weekends, showing them how to safely handle their own green horses and watching their confidence grow. She occasionally volunteers at local 4-H shows, helping kids learn safer leading and grooming techniques. On rare free evenings, she and Sam cook dinner together—often something simple like roasted vegetables and grilled chicken—and watch training videos or documentaries. Emily still attends clinics with more experienced mustang trainers when she can, taking notes in a battered spiral notebook and then methodically testing new ideas with her own horses. She doesn’t see herself as someone who “has it all figured out,” but rather as a working professional steadily refining her craft, one thoughtful, patient session at a time.

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just got home and finally sitting down with some tea, so I wanted to write while it’s quiet. It hit me this week that I’ve known Laura for just over seven years now. Feels wild thinking back to February/March 2019 when everything kind of crashed at once—Hazel moving to Jennings Creek, that awful depressive episode at CSU, and me slowly moving my stuff out of the house in Fort Collins.

I still remember Dr. Megan Lewis at the CSU counseling center telling me I needed to cut my course load and spend more time at the barn if I was going to crawl out of that hole. She was right. Those early mornings driving from your place to Jennings Creek in Loveland, just to hand-graze Hazel and breathe for a minute, probably kept me in school. That was right around when I wrapped up that stupid small-claims mess with the Greeley barn manager over the $2,300 in unpaid training fees and the January 2019 vet bill. I was so embarrassed about it then, but now it just feels like one more thing I got through.

A few months later, in June 2019, Sam and I moved into 2147 Jennings Creek Lane and I officially changed my mailing address and all the Social Security stuff. It felt like such a grown-up step, even though the townhouse still has your old kitchen table and Dad’s wobbly bookcase.

Anyway, I’m okay—tired, but okay. I’ll drive up for Sunday dinner like usual. Tell Dad I’ll bring fresh carrots for his “grandhorses.”

Love you,
Emily

metadata.subject: Catching up (and thinking about 2019)

artifacts.content:
Personal timeline / context notes – for me, not anyone else.

Early 2019 was such a pivot point. I met Laura through that local Mustang and burro advocacy group at the Larimer County Fairgrounds right after everything with Jake blew up. His DUI arrest in Denver in April 2018 shook all of us more than we said out loud. I think that’s part of why I threw myself so hard into volunteering with the mustangs and then into Hazel and, later, Rowan.

By March 2019 I’d moved Hazel from that Greeley barn, finally done with the small-claims settlement over the $2,300 training/vet mess. Around the same time I was still digging out emotionally from the depressive episode the previous semester—those sessions with Dr. Megan Lewis at the CSU counseling center in Fort Collins are still in my head when I feel myself sliding.

Then May–June 2019: moved in with Sam at 2147 Jennings Creek Lane, changed my mailing address, updated SSA records, and started boarding Hazel at Jennings Creek Equestrian. Sam was picking up extra ER shifts at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies so we could swing the $650/month board and training while I chipped away at the last $4,800 of my CSU loans.

Note to self: when I feel like I’m “behind,” remember I’ve already navigated depressive episodes, Jake’s DUI fallout, a court fight over $2.3k, and starting over in Loveland. I can handle a slow month of training clients.

Anyway, if you want a barn day, come up one Thursday–Sunday. I’m usually there from 7 a.m. to late afternoon, especially when I’m riding Rowan. We can grab coffee and talk about something other than engineering and horses for once.

metadata.title: Fire/cold weather planning session

artifacts.content:
Hi Laura,

I’ve been meaning to put some of this in writing for both of us, partly so I don’t forget details when things get hectic.

I still think about that February 2020 incident with the unstable boarder trying to climb into the wild mare’s 6-foot pen. Moving the mustang pens into the restricted-access zone behind the main barn, adding the keypad lock on the side gate, and putting cameras on the round pen and isolation pens were some of the best decisions we’ve made. I’m glad Sam looked over the emergency contact sheet afterward and helped us add clearer language about mental health crises and when to call 911 instead of you or me.

Same with the yearling that bolted toward County Road 20E in June 2023. I know it hurt to pull $4,800 from your savings for the extra no-climb fence and the double-gate system on the south side, but that plus the incident report with the Larimer County Sheriff’s Office probably saved us from something worse.

On the horse side, the facilities really proved themselves in March 2022 when Rowan flipped unloading in the gravel strip by the main barn. Having Dr. Hart from Equine Medical Center of the Rockies stitch him up safely in the 6-foot pen and rubber-matted wash rack, and then monitoring him for 48 hours without further drama, is exactly why I trust this place with wild ones.

And then in May 2024, when we brought in that super reactive TIP mustang with the history of flipping over a panel at that Greeley trainer’s, I’m still grateful Jake came down to inspect the 60-foot round pen. Catching that one compromised weld on the north side before I did any liberty work was huge.

Thanks for always backing me on the “safety first, even if it’s expensive” stuff.

Emily

metadata.subject: Facility safety thoughts (for our own sanity)

artifacts.content:
Journal – rough month reflections

May has always been weird, and May 2023 was next-level. Between the string of neglected horses coming through Jennings Creek and Sam coming home shattered after that traumatic pediatric code at UCHealth, I could feel myself fraying. I’m glad I actually followed through and booked three Wednesday afternoon sessions at Summit View Counseling in Fort Collins. On those days I only scheduled one quiet groundwork session with Rowan and no new client intakes, and I told owners straight up I needed to protect my focus and emotional bandwidth.

It reminded me of Fall 2021, when my own anxiety and panic attacks got so bad that Dr. Keller at Loveland Behavioral Health Clinic wrote that note saying I shouldn’t handle reactive horses alone for at least three months. Rowan stayed in full-time training at Jennings Creek from October 2021 through January 2022. Emily-the-trainer side of me kept him going; Emily-the-human watched weekly video updates from the couch, trying not to spiral. Those structured reintroduction sessions in January were the first time in a while I didn’t feel like my body was betraying me.

Note to self: it’s okay to set hard limits. When I’m exhausted (like February 2024, getting up at 4:30 a.m. to hand-walk that colicky mustang in the isolation pen for three hours while Sam was on her third overnight) or when Jake is staying with us because his depression is flaring, the horses still get what they need—but I have to get what I need too.

Action items: keep Wednesdays light; keep Summit View’s number handy; don’t wait until I’m shaking in the round pen to ask for help again.

metadata.title: Boundaries + mental health around the barn

artifacts.content:
Hi Dad,

Quick money/horse/life update, since those always seem tangled together.

We’re running a little tight again. One client is 60 days behind on board and training fees for a hard-keeping mustang mare, and of course I couldn’t bring myself to cut her ration. I ended up buying an extra $180 worth of better grass hay this week rather than downgrading her feed. At the same time, we’re about $300 short on the mortgage for the house on Jennings Creek Lane for November. I hate even typing that.

It’s not the first time horses and money have collided—remember the small-claims court mess in Weld County back in early 2019 with the Greeley barn manager and the $2,300 dispute? At least now I’m more proactive. With that pushy mare from Greeley last April, Laura and I put everything in writing: $600/month discounted training board, a clear payment plan, and a clause saying we could sell the mare to recoup costs if the owner defaulted. Four months later she’d caught up and the mare was turning into a kids’ lesson prospect, so it worked out.

Still, between that, the $1,200 overdue board I had to work off last summer, and the Subaru needing tires before winter, it feels like I’m always one colic or truck repair away from trouble. I’m not asking for a bailout (though if you’re able to float that $300 for a couple weeks, I’d be grateful); mostly I just wanted you to know I’m trying to make better business decisions than 2019 Emily.

Love you,
Em

From: Samantha "Sam" Leigh Porter <sam.porter@email.com>
To: Megan Ruiz <megan.ruiz@example.com>
Date: 2024-05-20
Subject: Why I trust Emily with the tough ones

Megan,

You asked why I’m so adamant about people sending their difficult horses to Emily, so here’s the context.

In **March 2023**, my 5‑year‑old BLM mustang flipped over backward with me. I ended up in the ER at **UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies** with a mild concussion and a **$3,200** bill. I was actually the ER nurse on duty until they pulled me off the floor. After that, my partner and I agreed I would only handle him under **Emily Rose Cartwright (trainer)**’s direct supervision at **Jennings Creek Equestrian** for at least **six months**. She rebuilt his groundwork from scratch, and by **October 2023** I was safely leading and loading him again without panic attacks. So yes, I would absolutely recommend her for unbroken or difficult horses.

Her ethics are a big part of it. In **March 2024**, when **Karen Miller – long-time training client** wanted a 30‑day “problem fixer” program for **Rusty – 10‑year‑old Quarter Horse gelding**, Emily refused to just take the **$1,200 training fee**. She sent Karen to **Dr. Alvarez at Front Range Equine Clinic, Loveland, CO** for a full lameness workup first; they confirmed hock and SI arthritis, and Emily declined to push him into the intensive 30‑day program.

Same thing in **October 2023** with **Mark Jensen – local contractor**. He offered **$2,000 cash** to stash **3 mustangs** at Jennings Creek for **60 days** with minimal handling; Emily not only turned him down but documented their condition at his northwest Loveland property on **October 12 and 14** and filed a report with **Larimer County animal control**.

That’s why I trust her.

Sam
